meta.title: TypeScript
meta.description: TypeScript type safety, generics, utility types, and compiler configuration

items.text: Is 'any' avoided in favor of specific types or 'unknown'?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Using 'any' disables type checking entirely. Use 'unknown' when the type is truly unknown and narrow it before use.
baseContent.whyItMatters: 'any' silently propagates through the type system, defeating the purpose of TypeScript. Bugs that types would catch go undetected.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for ': any', 'as any', and '<any>'. Each should be justified or replaced with a specific type or 'unknown'.
baseContent.exampleComment: This parameter is typed as 'any'. Use 'unknown' and add a type guard, or define the expected shape with an interface.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use 'any' as a convenience. Use 'unknown' + type narrowing when the type is genuinely unknown.

items.text: Is strict mode enabled in tsconfig.json?

baseContent.whatItMeans: strict: true enables strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, and other safety checks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without strict mode, TypeScript misses null/undefined errors, implicit any, and type coercion bugs — the most common sources of runtime errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check tsconfig.json for "strict": true. Verify individual strict flags aren't disabled (noImplicitAny: false, etc.).
baseContent.exampleComment: tsconfig.json has strict: false. Enable strict mode to catch null safety issues and implicit any types.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always enable strict mode. It's the baseline for meaningful type safety.

items.text: Are type assertions (as T) used sparingly and correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Type assertions (as T) override the compiler's type inference. They should only be used when you know more than the compiler.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect type assertions cause runtime errors that TypeScript can't catch. They're a promise to the compiler that you may break.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for 'as ' type assertions. Verify each one is necessary and correct. Prefer type guards or generics instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: This 'as User' assertion assumes the API returns a User, but there's no validation. Use a type guard or schema validation (zod).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer type guards over type assertions. If you must assert, validate the data first.

items.text: Are non-null assertions (!) avoided where possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The ! operator tells TypeScript a value is not null/undefined. If wrong, it causes runtime errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Non-null assertions silence the compiler without runtime checks. The value may actually be null, causing crashes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for '!' postfix on expressions. Replace with proper null checks, optional chaining (?.), or nullish coalescing (??).
baseContent.exampleComment: user.address!.city could crash if address is undefined. Use optional chaining: user.address?.city.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) instead of non-null assertions.

items.text: Do exported functions have explicit return types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Public API functions should have explicit return type annotations. Internal functions can rely on inference.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Explicit return types catch accidental changes to function contracts, improve documentation, and speed up type checking.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check exported functions for explicit return types. Internal/private functions can use inference.
baseContent.exampleComment: This exported function's return type is inferred as a complex union. Add an explicit return type for clarity.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add explicit return types to exported functions. Let TypeScript infer internal function types.

items.text: Are generics used where they add value (not over-engineered)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generics should be used when a function works with multiple types while preserving type relationships. Don't add generics for single-type functions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-generic code is harder to read and use. Under-generic code duplicates logic or uses 'any'.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if generic type parameters are actually used to relate inputs to outputs. A generic used only once may not need to be generic.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function has a generic T but only uses it as the return type. It could just return the specific type directly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use generics to preserve type relationships between inputs and outputs. Don't add them preemptively.

items.text: Are generic constraints (extends) used to limit acceptable types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use 'extends' to constrain generics: <T extends HasId> ensures T has an id property. Without constraints, generics accept anything.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unconstrained generics provide no type safety inside the function. You can't access properties without constraints.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check generic functions that access properties of T. They should have constraints ensuring those properties exist.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function accesses item.id but T has no constraint. Add <T extends { id: string }> to ensure id exists.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Constrain generics to the minimum shape they require. This enables type-safe property access.

items.text: Are built-in utility types used instead of manual type construction?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TypeScript provides Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, ReturnType, Parameters, and more. Use them instead of redefining.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Utility types are well-tested, widely understood, and reduce boilerplate. Manual construction is error-prone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for manually constructed types that match built-in utilities. Check for Partial<T> patterns written by hand.
baseContent.exampleComment: This type manually makes every field optional. Use Partial<User> instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Know the built-in utility types. They cover most type transformation needs.

items.text: Are discriminated unions used for variant types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use a shared literal property (discriminant) to distinguish union members. TypeScript narrows types automatically based on the discriminant.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Discriminated unions enable exhaustive checking. The compiler catches missing cases in switch statements.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check union types for a shared discriminant field. Verify switch/if statements on unions are exhaustive.
baseContent.exampleComment: This union has no discriminant. Add a 'type' field to each variant so TypeScript can narrow automatically.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add a discriminant field to union types. Use exhaustive switch statements to handle all variants.

items.text: Are interfaces and type aliases used consistently?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use interfaces for object shapes (extendable). Use type aliases for unions, intersections, and computed types. Be consistent.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing interfaces and types randomly makes the codebase inconsistent. Interfaces support declaration merging, types support unions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for consistency. If the project uses interfaces for objects, don't introduce type aliases for the same pattern.
baseContent.exampleComment: The project uses interfaces for data shapes but this new type uses a type alias. Use an interface for consistency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Be consistent. Use interfaces for shapes, type aliases for unions and computed types.

items.text: Are const enums or string unions preferred over regular enums?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Regular enums generate runtime code. String literal unions or 'as const' objects are often lighter and more type-safe.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Enums have quirks (reverse mappings, numeric default values) that cause subtle bugs. String unions are simpler.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for regular enums that could be string unions. Verify numeric enums are intentional, not accidental.
baseContent.exampleComment: This enum could be a string union: type Status = 'active' | 'inactive' | 'pending'. Simpler and no runtime overhead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer string literal unions for simple value sets. Use 'as const' objects when you need runtime values.

items.text: Are type guards used for safe type narrowing?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use typeof, instanceof, 'in' operator, or custom type guard functions (is keyword) to narrow types safely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Type narrowing gives you type-safe access to properties. Without it, you need unsafe type assertions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for type assertions that could be replaced with type guards. Look for 'as' casts after if/switch statements.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses 'as Dog' after checking the type. Use a type guard: function isDog(animal: Animal): animal is Dog.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use type guards instead of type assertions. The compiler narrows types automatically after guards.

items.text: Is Readonly<T> or readonly used for data that shouldn't be mutated?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mark properties, arrays, and parameters as readonly when they shouldn't be modified after creation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Readonly catches accidental mutations at compile time. It communicates intent and prevents bugs in shared data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for arrays or objects passed around that are mutated unexpectedly. Consider marking function parameters as readonly.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function modifies the input array. Mark the parameter as readonly to prevent this, or clone the array.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use readonly for data that shouldn't change. It prevents accidental mutations.

items.text: Are Promise return types explicit and error cases typed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Async functions should have explicit Promise<T> return types. Consider Result types or typed errors for expected failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untyped promise rejections are caught as 'unknown'. Without explicit types, callers don't know what errors to expect.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check async functions for explicit return types. Check catch blocks for proper error typing.
baseContent.exampleComment: This async function can throw three different error types but the return type doesn't indicate this. Consider a Result<T, E> pattern.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Type your async function returns explicitly. Consider Result types for functions with expected failure modes.

items.text: Are caught errors typed correctly (not assumed to be Error)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In TypeScript, caught errors are 'unknown' by default (with useUnknownInCatchVariables). Always narrow before accessing .message.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Not everything thrown is an Error. Strings, numbers, and objects can be thrown. Assuming Error causes runtime crashes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check catch blocks for direct access to err.message without checking instanceof Error first.
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch block accesses err.message directly. Check instanceof Error first: if (err instanceof Error) { ... }.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always narrow caught errors with instanceof before accessing Error properties.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Calling an async function without awaiting or handling the Promise is usually a bug. The error is silently swallowed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhandled Promise rejections crash Node.js processes and cause silent failures in browsers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for async function calls without await, .then(), or .catch(). Enable the @typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises rule.
baseContent.exampleComment: saveToDatabase(data) is called without await. If it fails, the error is silently lost. Add await.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always await or handle Promises. Use no-floating-promises ESLint rule to catch these automatically.

items.text: Are barrel exports (index.ts) not causing circular dependencies or bundle bloat?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Barrel files (index.ts re-exporting everything) can cause circular dependencies and prevent tree-shaking.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Circular dependencies cause undefined imports at runtime. Barrel files importing everything prevent bundlers from tree-shaking unused code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for circular import warnings. Verify barrel files aren't importing heavy modules that most consumers don't need.
baseContent.exampleComment: This barrel file re-exports everything including heavy utility functions. Import directly from the source module instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use barrel files sparingly. Import directly from source modules to enable tree-shaking.

items.text: Are path aliases configured consistently (tsconfig paths)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use tsconfig paths (@/components, @/utils) for clean imports. Ensure they match bundler/runtime configuration.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mismatched path aliases between tsconfig and bundler cause build failures. Inconsistent usage creates confusing imports.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that tsconfig paths match the bundler config (webpack resolve.alias, vite resolve.alias, etc.).
baseContent.exampleComment: This import uses a relative path ../../components but the project has a @/components alias. Use the alias for consistency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use path aliases consistently. Ensure tsconfig and bundler configs agree.

items.text: Are .d.ts files only used for third-party type declarations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Declaration files (.d.ts) should only be used for typing untyped third-party modules. Your own code should use .ts files.
baseContent.whyItMatters: .d.ts files are not type-checked against implementation. Types can drift from reality without any compiler warnings.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for .d.ts files that declare types for your own code. These should be regular .ts files.
baseContent.exampleComment: This .d.ts file declares types for your own API client. Move these to a regular .ts file so they're validated.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use .d.ts only for third-party module declarations. Your own types belong in .ts files.
